questions.question: Which manufacturing process removes material to create the final shape?
options: Casting (pouring molten material into a mold)
questions.explanation: Machining is subtractive manufacturing: material is removed by cutting tools. Includes: turning (lathe), milling, drilling, grinding. Produces high precision but generates waste (chips).

questions.question: Injection molding is economical for producing 10 custom parts.
questions.explanation: Injection molding has high tooling costs ($10K-$500K for molds) but very low per-part cost at volume. Economical at 1000+ parts. For 10 parts: use CNC machining, 3D printing, or vacuum casting.

questions.question: In CNC machining, the programmed path that the cutting tool follows is called the ___.
questions.explanation: The toolpath is generated from CAD geometry by CAM software. It defines every movement of the cutting tool: approach, cutting passes, retract. Expressed in G-code (G0: rapid, G1: linear cut, G2/G3: arcs).

questions.question: You need to manufacture 50,000 aluminum smartphone cases per month. Which process gives the best quality-cost-speed combination?

constraints: Complex internal features (antenna lines, button holes)

options: CNC machining from billet — highest precision but slow and expensive at volume
Die casting + CNC finish machining — fast casting for shape + precision machining for critical features
Extrusion + stamping — cheapest but can't achieve required tolerances
3D printing (DMLS) — flexible but far too slow for 50K/month
questions.explanation: Apple's approach: die-cast near-net-shape (fast, cheap at volume), then CNC machine critical surfaces and features (precision where needed). Combines die casting speed with CNC accuracy. Neither alone is optimal at this volume+precision.

questions.question: Match each manufacturing process to its ideal application:

right: High-volume thin parts (car body panels, brackets)
Complex geometries in metal (turbine blades, jewelry)

questions.explanation: Forging: grain flow alignment → strongest parts. Stamping: fastest for thin sheet parts (1000s/hour). Investment casting: nearly any geometry possible (lost-wax process). FDM: no tooling cost, design freedom.

questions.question: A CNC milling operation uses: spindle speed 8000 RPM, 4-flute end mill, feed per tooth 0.1mm. What's the feed rate (mm/min)?
questions.explanation: Feed rate = 8000 RPM × 4 flutes × 0.1 mm/tooth = 3200 mm/min. This determines how fast the tool traverses. Too fast: tool breakage, poor finish. Too slow: rubbing, heat buildup, poor productivity.
